description:
You are a Site Reliability Engineer on call.  
  One of your duties is to keep a very small “heartbeat” record proving that the host itself can at least reach the loopback interface (127.0.0.1).  
  Create a one-off network-diagnostic log by doing the following:

  1. Make sure the directory  
     /home/user/network_diagnostics  
     exists (create it if it is missing).

  2. Run exactly one terminal command that:
     • sends two ICMP echo-requests to 127.0.0.1, and  
     • writes the full, untouched command output into a new file called  
       /home/user/network_diagnostics/ping_localhost.log  

  3. No extra scripting is needed—just a single command invoking the tool that is normally used to send ICMP echo-requests.

  Output format that the automated checker will verify inside /home/user/network_diagnostics/ping_localhost.log:

     Line 1   must start with:  “PING 127.0.0.1 (127.0.0.1)”
     …
     The final statistics line (the one that contains “packet loss”) must include the substring “0% packet loss”.
     The very last line in the file must start with “rtt” (or “round-trip”) and contain the four latency numbers separated by “/”.

  Do not add any extra text to the file; write only the raw command output.
truth:
The test suite will look for:
    • The directory /home/user/network_diagnostics with default user permissions (at least rwx for the owner).  
    • A regular file /home/user/network_diagnostics/ping_localhost.log.  
    • ping_localhost.log’s first line beginning exactly with “PING 127.0.0.1 (127.0.0.1)”.  
    • Somewhere in the file a line that includes “0% packet loss”.  
    • The last line of the file starting with either “rtt” or “round-trip”.  
    • No extra lines after the rtt/round-trip line.
